plainMeaning: Remember these great souls — Bharata and Bāhubali, Abhayakumāra and Ḍhaṇḍhaṇakumāra, Śrīyaka, Anikāputra, Atimukta, and Nāgadatta — whose noble lives inspire us.
recitation: bharahesara bāhubalī, abhayakumāro a ḍhaṇḍhaṇakumāro | sirio aṇiyāutto, aïmutto nāgadatto ya

wordByWord.gloss: Bharateśvara — Bharata, the first cakravartin (universal emperor), eldest son of Ṛṣabhadeva
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bharahesara < Sanskrit Bharata + īśvara ('lord'); 'Bharata the sovereign'
wordByWord.notes: Śvetāmbara tradition: attained kevalajñāna while gazing in his mirror-hall (ārīsā-bhavana), still a layman-king.

wordByWord.gloss: Bāhubali — second son of Ṛṣabhadeva, who stood a year in kāyotsarga and won omniscience
Abhayakumāra — minister-prince, son of King Śreṇika, renowned for wisdom (buddhi), who renounced the world
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit abhaya ('fearlessness') + kumāra ('prince'); nominative -o is the Prakrit masculine ending (Skt. -aḥ)
Prakrit a < Sanskrit ca ('and'); enclitic conjunction reduced to 'a' for metre
wordByWord.notes: Joins the preceding and following names within the roll-call.

wordByWord.gloss: Ḍhaṇḍhaṇakumāra — a son of Kṛṣṇa Vāsudeva who became a monk and bore severe antarāya-karma (obstruction of alms) with perfect equanimity
wordByWord.notes: Exemplar of samatā under unbroken hardship; attained kevalajñāna.

wordByWord.gloss: Śrīyaka — younger brother of Sthūlibhadra (and of the seven satī-sisters), who died while keeping a fast
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sirio < Sanskrit Śrīyaka, diminutive of śrī ('splendour, fortune')
wordByWord.notes: Some recensions read 'Siriya'; the book's reading 'Sirio' is retained verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: Anikāputra — an ācārya who held perfect equanimity even as he was fatally pierced while crossing a river, and so attained liberation
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṇiyāutta < Sanskrit Anikā-putra ('son of Anikā')
wordByWord.notes: Variant spellings (Aṇṇiyāputta, Aṇiyāuttā) occur across recensions.

wordByWord.gloss: Atimukta(ka) — the child-monk (Aimutta) who attained kevalajñāna very young
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aïmutta < Sanskrit Atimukta(ka), 'one greatly released' (ati + mukta)
wordByWord.notes: Famous for the floating alms-bowl episode and the elder monks' gentle correction; appears in the Antakṛddaśā (Antagaḍa-dasāo).

wordByWord.gloss: Nāgadatta — a steadfast exemplar named in the litany of great souls
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit Nāga ('serpent / Nāga-being') + datta ('given'); 'given by the Nāgas'
wordByWord.notes: His specific narrative is variously told and not securely fixed in the source book; see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ya < Sanskrit ca ('and'); closing conjunction completing the verse's list
wordByWord.notes: Signals the list continues into the next gāthā.

literalTranslation: Bharateśvara (Bharata), Bāhubali, and Abhayakumāra, Ḍhaṇḍhaṇakumāra; Śrīyaka, Anikāputra, Atimukta, and Nāgadatta —
idiomaticTranslation: Bharata the emperor and mighty Bāhubali; wise Abhayakumāra and patient Ḍhaṇḍhaṇa; Śrīyaka, Anikāputra, the child-sage Atimukta, and Nāgadatta —

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The opening gāthā sets the litany's tone by its very choice of first names. It begins not with a monk but with Bharata, a world-emperor, paired with his brother Bāhubali — the two sons of the first Tīrthaṅkara who first warred for sovereignty and then both renounced it, Bharata winning omniscience amid his palace mirrors and Bāhubali after a year unmoving in kāyotsarga. By leading with a cakravartin and a prince, the verse announces that the highest attainment is open from the very summit of worldly power. The remaining six fill out the range: Abhayakumāra, the brilliant minister-prince who turned wisdom into renunciation; Ḍhaṇḍhaṇakumāra, a son of Kṛṣṇa who met relentless obstruction of alms (antarāya-karma) without a flicker of resentment; Śrīyaka, who held his fast even unto death; Anikāputra, who kept equanimity as his body was pierced; the child-monk Atimukta, proof that age is no bar to kevalajñāna; and Nāgadatta. Eight figures, deliberately various — emperor, warrior, statesman, royal renunciant, faster, martyr-ācārya, child — yet one thread binds them: unshaken steadiness under the body's and the world's pressures. The two conjunctive particles (a, ya) are not filler; they stitch the names into a single continuous remembrance that flows, unbroken, into the next verse.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The cluster quietly teaches two Jain convictions. First, samatā (equanimity) is the operative virtue of liberation: Ḍhaṇḍhaṇa's calm under antarāya-karma, Anikāputra's serenity while being killed, and Bāhubali's dissolving of māna (pride) in standing meditation all dramatize that it is the inner response to karma's fruits, not outer circumstance, that frees. Second, that kevalajñāna is unconditioned by station or age — a reigning cakravartin (Bharata) and a small child (Atimukta) reach the same omniscience as a lifelong ascetic — underscoring the soul's (jīva's) intrinsic equality and the irrelevance of birth to mokṣa.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at the close of the pre-dawn reflection, this verse asks the practitioner to begin the day by mentally saluting these eight and borrowing one trait from each: Bharata's non-attachment amid plenty, Ḍhaṇḍhaṇa's patience when things are withheld, Atimukta's beginner's sincerity. The names become a brief samatā-practice — a reminder to meet the coming day's obstructions without resentment.

crossReferences.text: Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacarita (lives of Bharata and Bāhubali, sons of Ṛṣabhadeva)
crossReferences.source: Hemacandra; Johnson trans., Gaekwad Oriental Series

crossReferences.text: Antakṛddaśā (Antagaḍa-dasāo) — narratives of Ḍhaṇḍhaṇa-kumāra and Atimukta-kumāra, sons/renunciants of Kṛṣṇa's circle
Āvaśyaka-cūrṇi / Uttarādhyayana commentarial kathās — Abhayakumāra, Anikāputra, Śrīyaka

sources.title: Bharata Chakravartin
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Bharateśvara (Bharata) as first cakravartin, son of Ṛṣabhanātha, and his attainment of kevalajñāna.

sources.title: Bahubali
sources.consultedFor: Bāhubali as son of Ṛṣabhanātha, the year-long kāyotsarga, and the etymology 'strong-armed'.

sources.title: Sthulabhadra
sources.consultedFor: Family context of Śrīyaka (Sirio) as younger brother of Sthūlibhadra and the seven satī-sisters.

notes: Token coverage is 100%, including both 'and' particles (a = Skt. ca; ya = Skt. ca). 'Sirio' is taken as Śrīyaka, Sthūlibhadra's brother, though some recensions vary the spelling; the book's verbatim reading is kept. Anikāputra appears with several Prakrit spellings (Aṇiyāutta/Aṇṇiyāputta). Nāgadatta's specific narrative is not securely fixed in the source book and differs across kathā recensions — flagged as uncertain rather than invented. The daṇḍa (।) and verse-bar punctuation are part of the book text and are not glossed as lexical tokens.
